statement: The dodo went extinct in less than 80 years of humans arriving on Mauritius.
wowFactor: One of the fastest human-caused extinctions ever recorded — gone in a single lifetime.
explanation: Humans first colonized Mauritius around 1638. The last confirmed dodo sighting was in 1681 — less than 45 years later. Some records suggest the final individuals may have survived until the late 17th century, but the species collapsed with shocking speed. The dodo had evolved in complete isolation on Mauritius with no land predators, so it had no fear of humans or the animals humans brought with them (dogs, rats, pigs). This fearlessness made them trivially easy to capture and kill.
gaiaComment: Forty-five years from discovery to extinction. Humans barely had time to name them before they were gone.
quizQuestion: How quickly did the dodo go extinct after humans arrived on Mauritius?
correctAnswer: In less than 80 years — the last confirmed sighting was only about 45 years after first human colonization
distractors: After about 500 years of slow decline
Almost immediately — within a few months of first contact
After about 1,000 years of coexistence with human hunters
It went extinct before humans arrived, from natural causes
After about 200 years of gradual population decline
In about 10 years due to a single epidemic
After exactly 100 years, coinciding with industrialization
It survived until the 20th century before finally going extinct

statement: The dodo was endemic to the island of Mauritius — it lived nowhere else on Earth.
wowFactor: An entire species existed on just one island — and we wiped it out.
explanation: Mauritius is a volcanic island in the Indian Ocean, about 2,000 km east of Madagascar. The dodo (Raphus cucullatus) evolved there in complete isolation over millions of years, adapting to the island's unique environment. It had no relatives on mainland Africa or nearby Madagascar — it evolved from pigeon-like ancestors that reached the island and then adapted to a flightless, ground-dwelling lifestyle. When Mauritius was colonized, the entire global population of dodos was concentrated on one small island with no escape possible.
gaiaComment: One island. One species. No backup population, no refuge. The math for extinction was always unforgiving.
correctAnswer: Only on the island of Mauritius — they were endemic and found nowhere else
distractors: Throughout Africa and the Indian Ocean islands
On multiple Indian Ocean islands including Madagascar and Reunion
In the rainforests of South America
On islands across the Pacific Ocean
In the wetlands of Southeast Asia
On the main continent of Africa near coastal regions
Throughout the islands of the Caribbean Sea
In Australia and the surrounding island chains

statement: The dodo was not actually stupid — it had no fear of humans because it had no land predators, making it seem tame.
wowFactor: The dodo's 'stupidity' was actually millions of years of successful island evolution.
explanation: The dodo evolved on Mauritius with no natural land predators. Its only predators were large birds of prey, and the dodo's behavior was adapted to defend against aerial threats (hiding in vegetation) rather than land threats. When humans arrived, the dodo had no behavioral response for ground-level predators and simply walked up to hunters. This was not stupidity — it was appropriate behavior for its evolutionary environment. Its brain-to-body ratio was actually comparable to its relatives. The 'dead as a dodo / stupid as a dodo' reputation is a posthumous insult by the very species that killed it.
gaiaComment: We called them stupid for trusting us. The failure of judgment was entirely on our side.
quizQuestion: Why did dodos appear 'stupid' and unafraid of humans?
correctAnswer: Because they evolved without land predators — fearlessness was correct behavior in their undisturbed habitat
distractors: Because they actually had very small brains relative to their body size
Because island isolation caused genuine cognitive decline over generations
Because they were too large and heavy to run away from any threat
Because they were partially deaf and couldn't hear approaching humans
Because they were nocturnal and humans always approached during the day
Because they were domesticated by early Malagasy sailors before Europeans arrived
Because their thick skull protected them, making fear unnecessary
Because they mistook humans for a harmless species they had coexisted with

statement: The dodo's closest living relative is the Nicobar pigeon, not any flightless bird.
wowFactor: The dodo is most closely related to a beautiful iridescent pigeon from Southeast Asia.
explanation: DNA analysis of dodo subfossil bones has confirmed that dodos belong to the pigeon family (Columbidae). Their closest living relative is the Nicobar pigeon (Caloenas nicobarica), a stunning, iridescent green and copper bird found on islands from the Andaman and Nicobar Islands to the Solomon Islands. The dodo is not closely related to ostriches, emus, or other flightless birds — it independently evolved flightlessness from a pigeon ancestor that colonized Mauritius. The related Rodrigues solitaire (also now extinct) was also in this group.
gaiaComment: The dodo's living cousin is a gorgeous metallic pigeon. The family tree has beautiful branches.
quizQuestion: What is the dodo's closest living relative?
correctAnswer: The Nicobar pigeon — an iridescent pigeon from Southeast Asia and Pacific islands
distractors: The common rock pigeon (city pigeon)
The ostrich — both are large flightless birds
The emu — the only other truly flightless island bird
The kiwi from New Zealand
The cassowary from New Guinea
The kakapo, the flightless parrot of New Zealand
The penguin — both evolved flightlessness in isolation
The flamingo — both have similar leg structure

statement: Dodos weighed about 10-18 kg — similar to a large turkey or small goose.
wowFactor: They weren't the massive waddling giants that old illustrations suggest.
explanation: Early depictions of dodos (mostly from the late 1600s) show them as extremely obese, round birds, but modern analysis of skeletal remains suggests the real dodo was leaner — particularly in the wild. Estimates put wild dodo weight at 10-17 kg for males. The obese depictions may reflect captive individuals that were overfed aboard ships. A wild dodo was more likely a compact, muscular bird adapted for walking through dense forest. It stood about 1 meter tall and had a large head with a hooked beak adapted for hard food like nuts and seeds.
gaiaComment: Leaner and more capable than the cartoon blob of popular imagination. History was unkind in every way.
correctAnswer: About 10-18 kg — similar to a large turkey, and leaner than old illustrations suggest
distractors: About 100 kg — as heavy as a small lion
About 1 kg — smaller than a chicken
About 50 kg — as heavy as a large dog
About 200 kg — nearly the size of a small cow
About 5 kg — similar to a large domestic cat
About 500 kg — a massive flightless bird like a moa
About 30 kg — three times the weight of a turkey
About 2 kg — a small island-adapted lightweight bird

statement: The last confirmed dodo sighting was in 1681 — and the species was not formally described scientifically until 1865.
wowFactor: The dodo went extinct before science even officially defined what it was.
explanation: The dodo was last reliably reported alive in 1681. The species was not formally described in the Linnaean scientific system (with a proper Latin binomial name) until 1865 — 184 years after it had already gone extinct. This happened because early naturalists were uncertain about the bird's classification and the early reports were considered possibly exaggerated. It is one of the few animal species humanity encountered, exterminated, and then officially categorized only as a historical artifact — described posthumously from collected bones and old drawings.
gaiaComment: Extinct for 184 years before we gave it a proper scientific name. We catalogued the ghost.
quizQuestion: When was the dodo formally described and given a scientific name?
correctAnswer: In 1865 — 184 years after the species had already gone extinct
distractors: In 1638, when Dutch sailors first landed on Mauritius
In 1681, the year the last individual was seen
In 1700, shortly after extinction was recognized
In 1850, based on a recently discovered live specimen
In 1800, during the Linnaean classification boom
In 1950, after DNA analysis of bones was perfected
In 1750, when the first complete skeleton was assembled
In 1900, when Mauritius became a British colony

statement: The dodo was driven extinct by a combination of hunting, habitat destruction, and introduced animals — not just by humans eating them.
wowFactor: The real killers were the rats and pigs humans brought with them.
explanation: European sailors did hunt and eat dodos, but the more devastating impact came from the animals they introduced to Mauritius: pigs, goats, dogs, cats, rats, and macaques. These invasive species raided dodo nests (which were on the ground) and ate eggs and chicks. Habitat destruction from forest clearing further reduced the dodo's range and food supply. The combination of hunting, nest predation, and habitat loss created a multi-front assault on a species already vulnerable due to low population size and no evolved defenses.
gaiaComment: Sailors get the blame. The rats did much of the work. Extinction is rarely a single cause.
quizQuestion: What were the main causes of the dodo's extinction?
correctAnswer: A combination of hunting, introduced animals (rats, pigs, dogs) raiding nests, and habitat destruction
distractors: Exclusively overhunting by Dutch and Portuguese sailors
A sudden disease epidemic that killed all individuals rapidly
Climate change that destroyed their food supply
Competition with a native predator that suddenly expanded its range
Volcanic eruption that covered the island in ash
Habitat loss alone — they were never significantly hunted
Interbreeding with introduced pigeon species causing genetic collapse
Inability to find food after a major drought cycle

statement: Dodos ate fallen fruit, seeds, nuts, bulbs, and possibly small invertebrates and crabs.
wowFactor: Their hooked beak was a precision tool for cracking hard seeds — they weren't just waddling around eating whatever.
explanation: The dodo's large, hooked beak was well-suited for opening hard fruits and nuts. Analysis of dodo skull mechanics suggests a powerful bite capable of cracking large, tough-shelled fruits — likely the large, hard fruits of Calvaria trees (also called the tambalacoque tree). There is a famous (though disputed) hypothesis that the Calvaria tree depended on dodos to pass its seeds through their digestive system before germination. Dodos also likely ate bulbs, roots, and possibly small animals. They were omnivores in the tropical forest environment of Mauritius.
gaiaComment: A hooked beak built for cracking open the forest's hardest fruits. Precision engineering in a bird called stupid.
correctAnswer: Fallen fruits, hard seeds and nuts, bulbs, and possibly small invertebrates — cracked open with their powerful hooked beak
distractors: Exclusively fish from coastal waters
Only leaves and grass from the forest floor
Mainly other birds' eggs and chicks
Filter-feeding from shallow coastal waters
Exclusively carrion from dead animals
Marine plankton and algae
Only seeds from a single specific tree species
Insects caught by rapid aerial pursuit

statement: The dodo nested on the ground in a simple nest of grasses — completely vulnerable to introduced predators.
wowFactor: Their nesting strategy, perfect for an island without predators, became their fatal flaw.
explanation: Like many island birds that evolved without ground predators, dodos nested directly on the ground in simple grass structures. On Mauritius before human arrival, this was a perfectly sensible strategy — there were no land mammals that could threaten a nest. When Europeans arrived with rats, pigs, cats, dogs, and macaques, the dodo's ground nests were exposed to predators they had never evolved to deal with. A single breeding failure per year, repeated over decades, was enough to collapse the population.
gaiaComment: Ground nest, no predators — a perfectly reasonable strategy for three million years. Then everything changed at once.
quizQuestion: Why were dodo nests particularly vulnerable to introduced species?
correctAnswer: They nested on the ground with no defensive behavior against land predators — evolved on an island without any
distractors: Because they built elaborate elevated nests easily found by sailors
Because they nested in large colonies, making all eggs easy to find at once
Because their nests were bright white and visible from far away
Because they made loud calls that attracted predators to their nest sites
Because they nested near human settlements to stay warm
Because they only nested during daytime, allowing easy observation
Because nest sites were revealed by their distinctive footprints in mud
Because dodo parents would abandon nests rather than fight threats

statement: Scientists are actively working on sequencing the complete dodo genome as a step toward possible de-extinction.
wowFactor: The most famous extinction in history might be the first reversal.
explanation: In 2022, the colossal Biosciences team (the same group working on mammoth de-extinction) announced partnership with researchers at the Natural History Museum in London to sequence the complete dodo genome. High-quality dodo DNA has been extracted from subfossil bones. The plan would involve editing the genome of the Nicobar pigeon (the dodo's closest living relative) to reintroduce dodo-like traits. The ethical and ecological challenges are significant — modern Mauritius has a completely altered ecosystem from the dodo's time — but the genomic work is real and progressing.
gaiaComment: The species we most regret losing may be the first one we bring back. Science as apology.
quizQuestion: What makes dodo de-extinction a scientific possibility?
correctAnswer: Scientists have extracted high-quality DNA from subfossil bones and identified the Nicobar pigeon as the closest living relative for genome editing
distractors: Because dodo eggs preserved in amber have been found in Mauritius
Because a small population was discovered living in remote Mauritius forests in 2018
Because the dodo genome is identical to common pigeons — no editing needed
Because a frozen dodo specimen was found perfectly preserved in Antarctica
Because dodo DNA has been fully reconstructed from ancient paintings alone
Because laboratory cloning of dodo cells was already completed in 2020
Because dodo de-extinction was successfully completed in 2024
Because dodo genes were found preserved inside a museum taxidermy specimen
